reason: Both correctly diagnose the Goodhart/averaging problem and converge on the same core fixes (SLO burn, incident frequency/MTBF, total downtime, percentile TTR, pages per shift, recurrence rate, and the same count-vs-impact diagnostic). The two are largely equivalent, but B includes one genuinely new mechanism A lacks: 'clock-start drift' — the observation that MTTR can look better simply because 'resolved' now means mitigation rather than full fix, or because the incident clock starts later (detection delay), shrinking the measured window. This is a distinct gaming vector beyond severity/ticket reclassification and directly explains how the same metric misleads. B also operationalizes this insight by recommending measuring time-to-detect and time-to-mitigate separately from customer-impact start, which is actionable and not just restating A's points. This is a real, non-padding addition that would change what the asker investigates. Aside from this, the answers are comparable in clarity and structure.
